summary:
The Romanian Revolution of December 1989 was the only violent transition from communism in Eastern Europe during the wave of revolutions that swept the continent that year. Unlike the peaceful transitions in Poland, Czechoslovakia, and East Germany, the Romanian revolution involved armed conflict between protesters and regime forces, resulting in over 1,000 deaths. The uprising began in Timișoara on 16 December 1989, initially in defense of an ethnic Hungarian pastor, and rapidly spread to Bucharest.

Dictator Nicolae Ceaușescu, who had ruled Romania since 1965 through one of Eastern Europe's most repressive and personality-cult-obsessed regimes, initially attempted to crush the protests. However, the army turned against him, and on 22 December he fled the capital by helicopter with his wife Elena. Both were captured within days, given a brief military trial, and executed by firing squad on 25 December 1989 — the only communist leader to be executed during the revolutions of 1989.

The revolution's rapid course and the obscure circumstances of some of the violence have generated continuing historical controversy. Questions remain about the extent to which the revolution was a genuine popular uprising versus a coup by factions within the Romanian Communist Party. Romania's transition to democracy proceeded more slowly and with more difficulty than those of other Eastern European states.
